speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about life in Lwow. And how did you-- for nine months you were there. How did you manage to keep your-- your grievances of being away from your father-- how did you-- what did you do with your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard. This part was very, very difficult. Because I missed my father and my brother something awful. I really missed them. And I missed my grandfather and my grandmother, the whole [NON-ENGLISH]. I missed her very much. And what we did-- I think I had a little bit of education, in there, but privately-- you know, some friends of my uncle. And my uncle tried to teach me, because he was a very learned man. And he tried to teach me. But my heart was not in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not-- I preferred to go to the cinema. I preferred to go to the theater. And I used, you know, to go to just look around what's happening. And I was reading. That's one thing which my passion was, of reading. I was doing a lot of reading, but otherwise, nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lwow. In Lwow came a new decree. The Russians had the decree again. We give you two options. And I'm talking about 300,000 people. 300,000 Jewish people were there-- you know, refugees from Poland. Either you take Russian citizenship, so you can remain in Lwow. Or if you don't want to take Russian citizenship, it's up to you. We're not going to kill you because of that. Then you've got to go back, where you come from, to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, we had a conference, you know, and my family was [INAUDIBLE] and my uncle. And I said, look, I don't like either option. I don't-- for sure we're not going back. As much as my heart was aching to see my father and my brother, you know, we're not going to go back. That's not-- for sure I'm not going back, all right? And we don't going to take a Russian citizenship, because you don't know what it's going to lead to. We don't to be-- we don't want to be Russians citizens, all right? | had a mind of my own. I really had a mind of my own. I-- How-- if I may ask, how were Jews being treated during this period in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians? They just left it alone. They didn't bother us. We didn't bother them. They didn't bother us. They didn't feel that they are occupying the country, you know, they're occupying Lwow. They felt that this is only a short lived period. Because they also knew that there's going to be a war between Russia and Germany, that this was only a bandage sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were not very interested in what's going on in there. They didn't improve the city. They didn't want to do anything in the city. You know, everything was dilapidated, or they let it go, you know. They left us alone. We left them alone. So you were given the options?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were given the option. So we didn't accept it. And so they made a list of people. You know, they had a list. Anyway, whoever registered that he wants a Russian citizenship, so they got Russian citizenship. Whoever-- there was some people going back. Thousands went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one night-- actually, three nights, three nights. And I think we were caught on the second night. They came. The Russians always do-- you know, in those days, when the commies were in power, you know, and [INAUDIBLE] especially, their methods was doing things, everything, at nighttime. When it was dark, nobody saw anything. In the morning, you woke up, nothing happened. Right? Nothing happened. So in three nights, they were able to take 300,000 people to the railway station in Lwow, into cattle trains, and into Russia, deep into Russia. I think we were caught in the second night. We were caught in the second night. And again, they did not divide the families, all right? My uncle, my auntie, the two boys-- you know, the two cousins-- Morris and Jan, and myself and my mother, and by coincidence-- by coincidence, there was a family in there. And when we were in Krakow, in the bombardment, and we were in the [INAUDIBLE] there, so we met them there. And they were on the same-- in the same wagon together there with us. And this woman is still alive today. You know, she's in New York. And I see her. Do you remember that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Lwow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, they took us-- it was in the middle of the night. I was sleeping like hell, you know? And the Russians walked in, very-- you know, with guns, with these-- they used-- they had you know, these-- not machine guns. They never had machine guns, but these big guns. And on top of it, that [NON-ENGLISH]. You know [NON-ENGLISH]? That's bayonets. And the bayonets are not wide, but they're thin. And I'll always remember those bayonets. You know, they're slim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [RUSSIAN]. And we couldn't speak Russian, of course. But we understood, because it's Slav language. And we were there nine months, you see, so already. [RUSSIAN]. And they took us. There was trucks waiting down on the floor, on the bottom. They didn't push anybody. They didn't do anything wrong-- into the wagon, onto the station. Into the wagons, right? And then when it was full, the train left the station. And we didn't know where we're going. We asked them, where we going.

speaker: interviewee
text: [RUSSIAN]. You know, you'll see. And we were going. And so we were traveling four weeks. Four weeks we were traveling. On the way, of course, the train stopped. And there was-- you know, they gave us food, and a bit hot soup on the way, and four weeks. And we went as far as the Urals, deep into the Urals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember the day we got out of the train. And they-- and so, where are we? And you know, it was a small city. It was a small little city there, you know, a township. And they said, no, no no. We're not staying in here. We're going to go-- everything is prepared for you inside, a little bit inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: The little bit inside was 100 kilometers away. And again, by trucks, on foot, you know, walking a little bit, a bit on the truck-- there was no beatings. There was no pushing. You know, it was just disorganized, if you can call it. But we were not under any threat. And we arrived to the city called Korostelovka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard, because it's Russian. But in English, it would be something like K-O-R-E-S-T-O-W-K-A, Korostelovka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it exactly that you arrived here? Do you know the date, around?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember the date. It was summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: In summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: In summer. And it was beautiful. The trees, the air-- magnificent. And we came in. And there was huts. All right, there was huts prepared. And there was a-- so there was a few rows of huts, 500 people. And inside the huts, there was-- in the one end there was a stove. And there was [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the wooden beds, you know, one next to the other practically, a little bit over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother and I were lying, you know, one next to the other, and my uncle, my auntie, and the two boys next, 50 people to a hut. And down in the bottom-- it was very picturesque, very beautiful. Down on the bottom was a little river, you know a tiny little river. And it was under the auspices-- there was-- the guards which where there was not ordinary police, you know, civil police. It was the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the [NON-ENGLISH] there was not only [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the-- the what's the name? The department which looked after spies-- you know, there was the secret police. And why we were under the secret police? Because-- because we didn't want to take Russian citizen, therefore we were spies. We were enemies of the country, enemies of Russia. The Jewish people-- enemies of Russia. You know, and that's why we were under their control. How long would you have spent in this encampment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the war started between Germany and Russia. It was, I think, another year-- actually, 14 months. I can remember now, 14 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what was the job in there? The job in there was that they took the people-- and we were laughing. At the first day, we were laughing. Because well, there was a lot of doctors, and a lot of accountants, and lot of business people. They never saw a axe in their hand. They never saw a saw in their hand-- took them into brigades, six people to a brigade, into the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russian-- not brought the experts, you know, from-- wood [INAUDIBLE], you know, to-- and to cut down the huge trees, and then to trim it, and then to cut it in-- what, like, two meters or something, and then stack it. And then, when the winter is over, and the winter-- the water is swollen with water-- the river is swollen with water, so they bring it down. And they floated, I don't know how long-- how far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's a huge factory in there, which makes [? coke ?] out of it. And it's like coal, you know, wooden coal. And that's used for all sort of things-- factories, and all sort of things, domestic and industrial. And that was their job. And I remember that first day very well, because everybody went to the forest laughing. You know, how are they like to cut wood? They've never seen a tree in their lives. They've never see an axe in their life. They were shouting at them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their laughter was stopped immediately, because the first tree, or the first few trees were [INAUDIBLE], it fell down. And it went straight onto a young girl, 18-year-old girl, and killed her on the spot. So then they saw that this is serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to do this work as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I didn't do it-- I was there because I was watching what the people were doing. I went with the-- it was two kilometers away from the village, right? And there, it was hunger. It was hunger. We were getting a meager you know, bread-- very meager bread-- you know, I don't know, 200 grams or something a day. And the bread was full of sand, you know. And there was nothing to buy. You couldn't buy anything. They didn't bring anything. You were way-- nobody was running away from there. Where are you going to run?

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no barbed wire around it. And what I remember is in Karlovka, most of all, there was two things I remember. The commandant had-- across the river, he had nets to catch fish, right? So I went with a friend of mine. I always had friends. You know, my age group, I always had friends. So I went in there and I was stealing the fish. Because it was across the river. He had, you know, such things, you know, to catch the fish. And I was seeing the fish. And once he caught us. And what could he do with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: He put us to jail. There was a jail in the camp. And I was there overnight. And he said, once more you do it, he said, you'll go to the big jail. That's one thing I remember. The next week, what I remember is, there was a man called Wulkan. That's the fellow-- that's the family, which I was in Krakow, in the shelter, when it was bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wulkan, W-U-L-K-A-N. Wulkan. And he had a wife. And he had two daughters. And he had a son. One of the daughters was a good friend of mine. She was older than me a little bit but she was a real friend of mine. And I was very, very fond of them, of that whole family. And he took it up upon himself to teach the young kids in my age group Yiddish [INAUDIBLE]. And there was no books, right-- from his head. Pesach came. So he invited me to come to his place for Pesach, you know, to read the Haggadah, you know, and to participate. And I will never forget it, that evening, because again, I went to the river. And this time I didn't steal, but I was catching some fish. And I brought it to Mrs. Wulkan. And Mrs. Wulkan made fish, something out of it. You know, she made something out of it. There was-- you know, there was-- for some reason, they had a small hut. There was only a few families in there. And there was a stove in the middle in there. And she was cooking in there, that-- the fish. And we were saying the Haggadah, [HEBREW]. He was explaining to us. He was a great man. You know, he was-- yeah, he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. And then now come time for, to eat. And she took the fish and put it on the table. On the way, she fell, and the fish fell down the floor. At that time, I made myself a mental note. And I promised myself that from now on, wherever I'll be, if I'll live through this thing, that Pesach will be strict, in my home, wherever I live. Till today, Pesach in our home is like in a rabbi's place. Everything is locked, and everything is shut, and [INAUDIBLE]. Everything is [INAUDIBLE] and everything is so kosher that you've got no idea. And I love it. It was from that moment. This is what I remember from there. And I also remember when the war finished-- when the war started, between the Russia and Poland-- and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, were you able to communicate out from where you were, with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a most interesting story. And I don't think that anybody has got anything like it. I don't think so. I don't know of anyone. As I told you, I was very perceptive. I was very quick, All right, and I was very observant. And I saw there were [INAUDIBLE] over there, fellows. There was a guy in there, which was somehow a little bit more humane than the others-- not that the others one were so terrible bad, but they did their job, right-- and that's it. But I saw him that he was a little bit more humane.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to my mother, you know what we're going to do? We're going to bribe him and buy him some vodka. And I got a bottle of vodka. And I said to him, listen. I've got a request of you. There's no mail coming into Korostelovka, right? The nearest post office is 100 kilometers away. I said, where do you actually go after you finish, you know, working here, you know, when you go-- for two, three days he goes, has a break, you know, when he's changed-- the guards are changing. He said, I live in such and such a city. I've got it written down somewhere. I've forgotten the name-- 100 kilometers away from this place where I'm working. This is only a workplace. So I said, in that case, let me give you a letter to my father. That was before the start of the war between Russia and Germany. And I'll pay you whatever it costs. Send it. Send it. I said to my mother, let's see what happens. And we wrote the letter to my father. And I said, when it comes back, when we get a letter back, for our name-- and I wrote him down the name, Weinreich, and so on-- you bring here to me. And then you get another bottle of whiskey-- of vodka.

speaker: interviewee
text: To our greatest shock and surprise, a letter came from my father, from Chrzanow. And that was the first contact we had from Chrzanow. And I have got those letters. And we found out through his letters that my brother was one of the first to be taken to the school, where I was going to school, where he was going to school, with a lot of boys his age group, which was 19, 20, 19-year-old, 20-year-old. And they were taken to Germany to work. And my brother was writing to his father cards-- not letters, but cards. And my father put the cards into his letters together and sent it us, to Korostelovka, and I've got them. And this is priceless to me, absolutely priceless.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother's reaction when she saw those letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shock. She was in absolute shock. I was trembling. And she was shocked. And that went on for quite some time. I got those letters and I got those cards. I translated it to-- in English for my children so that they can understand it. And that was something so incredible. Because I have got a letter from my father, who farewells me, because he knew he was going to go to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that I've got. And now those letters I knew by heart, every word. And I used to keep in my pocket, right through the war, even in Australia, when I was here the first, I think, six months. And I can't tell you with what-- with what holiness, you know, I look at those letters. It's just incredible, just-- you know, what he writes in there, with so much wisdom, and with so much understanding, and with so much heart, how he foresaw what is going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he writes to me two things-- to go to the rabbi and learn Torah, so that we are continuity. If your mother hasn't got the money, so you learn with your friends. Be diligent, so you'll be a good man. And he writes to me, be in contact with your two uncles, because you're going to migrate to them, all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the last letter he wrote, be respectful to your mother. I've got it. I'll show it to you later on. This is-- this is really incredible thing. And those-- those letters actually kept me alive. You know, right through the war, those letters kept me alive. You know, it's just incredible, and how he writes in those letters that he feels [NON-ENGLISH]. He is like-- he's as lonely as a stone. And he said, with the last drop of blood, he writes with these letters. You know, and he wants to impart his love-- how much he loved me when I was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how my brother writes to him cards and he says to him-- he says to him, send me some tobacco. Send me some cakes. Send me some bread. Send me back the clothes, which I sent you for cleaning, all right? And it-- you know, those cards and those letters are something so incredible. And we got them until, of course, the time when the war started between Russia and Germany. And I can almost pinpoint when my father went to Auschwitz. Because what they did there-- it's not like in Krakow they had a ghetto. The whole city was a ghetto. There was no ghetto in Chrzanow, right? The whole city was a ghetto. As they took the whole lot and sent them to Auschwitz. Now the young people, they took first. My brother was working on the railway station in Germany. In Germany he was working in a railway station. And he writes to his mother, I've got it so good in here. I'm so well, because you-- otherwise, the censors wouldn't let it through. You know, and I'm working, helping the railway to run, you know? And how long did you stay in this lager for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, till the war start-- till the war started between Russia and Germany. And then they realized, of course, that we are not spies. That was number one. And number two, they had a colossal source of manpower if they freed us. They freed us, so they could take the men to the army. But they couldn't take them to the Russian army, because they weren't Russian citizens. So they made a Polish army, under a Polish general, which was the biggest anti-Semite you can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in the lager till about June of--

speaker: interviewee
text: June '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: --'41 And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So the war finished. The war started between Germany and Russia and they said, you're free to go. You can't go towards the front, right? You can go inland, wherever you choose. Come to us. Tell us where you want to go. And we'll provide transport for you, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So the [INAUDIBLE]. So of course, my uncle-- as I told you, I was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, I was always doing something, you know? So I said to my uncle, listen. Where are we going? I tell you where we're going. Because I knew geography very well. I liked geography. We're going to go south. We're going to go-- there's a place called Samarkand. And we're going. It's a big city. And it's not far from the Persian border. That time it was Persia, right? And maybe we can cross the border. And from there we'll go to Palestine. Through Iran-- or Persia at the time-- right, we'll go to-- [INAUDIBLE]. I said, look, first of all, the weather will be better-- kinder. It's warmer. It's not in the Urals. I didn't tell you, but in the Urals it was bitterly cold. But nobody was sick in there, because there was no germs. Right, there was no germs. It was so cold that even the germs got killed. So warmer-- and there will be food. You know, something must be growing in there, right? I don't know what was growing in there, but it's growing, you know? So let's go south. And we got a-- so we went to authorities in there. And we said we going to Tashkent. They said, good. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, whatever they give us, some pieces of paper so we can go on a train for nothing. But of course, the train was very erratic. And of course, they-- you know, everything was give for the army. And all the trains, and every-- all the transports get to go to the front line. So we went on the train. It took us three weeks. And we finally came to a place, Kyzylorda, which is in Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kyzylorda? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-Z-Y-L cross, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dash?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dash, orda-- O-R-D-A. Kyzylorda, in Kazakhstan. That's not Kazakhstan. You know, this is not the [INAUDIBLE]. This is [? Kazach. ?] It's a big republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure-- K-A-Z-A-C-H-T-A-N [? Kazakhstan. ?] That's from the Russian I am spelling it, right? So when we arrived after three weeks in Kyzylorda, the children were exhausted. And my mother was exhausted. My auntie was exhausted. So I said to my uncle, let's get off the train in here-- the bus or whatever-- the truck in here. Because we're going by-- somewhere by truck, somewhere by train, you know, wherever we could. And let's stay here, I said, for three, four weeks, catch our breath, get our strength up. And then we'll go down to Tashkent, which is another 600 kilometers. And we got off in Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- is that a large city, Kyzylorda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite. It's a quite large city. It is-- it is about 250,000 people, I would say. And it's-- it is populated by [NON-ENGLISH]. They are [NON-ENGLISH]. They're not [? Kazakhis, ?] Kazakh. They have got eyes like that, yellow face-- yellow skin, very aristocratic, you know, tall people. And with-- you know, what hit me, you know, the first time when I saw them-- they were wearing heavy clothes in summer, you know, with big hats, you know, and heavy clothes in summer. I said, why? Against the sun? They were warm-- they were cooler under the-- hot-- on the warm clothes. And we were just the opposite, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, these people-- they were Muslims, right? And of course, in this city was a colossal influx of people, which were running from the war zone-- Russian Jews and Polish Jews. And on top of it, there was lots of nationalities in there-- Koreans, Uzbeks, what's the name? Koreans-- you had the Koreans, Uzbek, Chinese, you know. From all the regions around, they were congregating in [? Kazakh ?] because that was-- in Kyzylorda, because it was a city where there was-- a [INAUDIBLE] city, you know, where the people were doing things in there. You know, there was a market.

speaker: interviewee
text: On top of it, it had the largest-- absolutely the largest rice factory in the whole of Russia. It was so large. They had all American machinery, which was bought before the war. And they were so large that apparently, this factory was feeding 10% of the Russian army in rice. So you can imagine the scope of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in this city for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a long time. We stayed there-- then of course, we did not go any more to Tashkent. Because we were able somehow to establish ourselves in there, you know, put our roots in there. And we gave up the idea of going to Tashkent. And we also came reports from Tashkent that there is a huge influx of people in there, nowhere to live, nothing. You know, you're worse off in Tashkent that you are in here. There's less people in here than there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you set up a life in Kyzylorda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live? Where do you live? Now, so the first question was where are you going to live? Right, we didn't know anybody. I didn't know any Kazakhs. So again, I was very quick. And my uncle was tremendous. He found, actually, in the city, not far-- you know, when I talk about the city, I'm not talking about Sydney, you know. They're little huts, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, it was made out of [NON-ENGLISH], you know, out of mud-- mud houses, you know. Except a few houses, like the school, had bricks. You know, the police station had bricks. The library had bricks. But otherwise, everybody was living in-- it was ingenious. They-- I'll explain to you what it was. So he found two rooms in the city. And we couldn't-- so at that time, I said, it's enough that we've lived together for such a long time, right? Now let's see whether we can split, whether we can have something our own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought myself, look. I'll go the furthest place away from the city, and see what I can find. So I went to the furthest place. There was a railway station, a railway line going across. And I went across the railway. And I went to the last house on the other side. And there was also [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I go. And I knock at the door. There was a huge dog. And I said, look. We just arrived. And we want-- my mother and I, we want somewhere to live, right, where to sleep. Can give us something? We'll pay you, you know? And it was Russians-- you know, a lady, 60, you know, and her husband. And she said, look. I have got no room. But if you-- they all had [NON-ENGLISH], you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, there was a house, and there was a front yard. And there was a house that way, and a house that way. So it was like a little square. And with the front door, you know, you couldn't get in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so on the left hand side, he said, there's a cow in there. And we can divide that in half and you can live in there with your mother. Fine. Terrific. So we took over these premises. And the cow was on one side and we were on the other side. And there was a little wall in between. And all we had in there was-- the man, you know, the fellow made a [NON-ENGLISH], again, two [INAUDIBLE], and put straw on it. And we had a little bit of luggage, one little window, this size, looking outside onto the street, right, and one window looking into the yard, to their house. And their house was a palatial house-- beautiful curtains, white [? yarn. ?] I saw the curtains. I thought I was going mad. Curtains, with chairs, with-- decked in white, in cloth, and all sort of things. And the people were very nice to us. And we lived in there, together with the cow. And then a couple came. A couple came-- a brother and sister, begging us. They've got nowhere to live. We got them in there too! So we lived four people in a place of this size, right, in four [INAUDIBLE]. And we had a stove in the middle. And I was studying at the window, you know, at the window with a lamp from-- you know, from-- with a wick, you know, with a bit of kerosene on the bottom. And that's how we were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do on a day-to-day basis there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, in there-- so the first thing which I was looking for was education. So not very far from where we lived was a school, a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, it's a-- I don't know. What do you say in English? A school of-- no, to learn a trade, right? So I enrolled in there. And of course, I couldn't speak any-- a word of-- I couldn't Russian. I could not speak Russian. Because I couldn't learn-- in the camp, I didn't learn Russian. I learned Yiddish. And I went to school in there. But I must say, I was very fast. And I learned very quickly. Because the language is similar, you know, because it's a Slav language. So from Polish to Russian was not so bad. But the writing was bad, you know, and so on. But I learned it. And I learned it very quickly. And I learned it to perfection, actually. And that's what I did in Kyzylorda, when I was in the technical college. Then, after being in the technical college for some time-- I don't know in that-- together with the cow there, you know, for six, seven months, or maybe more, I found a place-- actually, I didn't find it. My uncle found it. Opposite him, where he lived in the city, he found a little place-- again, one room, less the cow, right, one room. And we grabbed it. And we went to the other side. So there it was more civilized and so on. And there, I enrolled into a proper school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [NON-ENGLISH], The Men's School of the name of Lenin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you learn there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There I learned everything-- Russian, history, geography, geometry, everything else. And also, they provided for us-- I don't know why, and I don't know how. They provided for us a teacher, which was teaching us Polish, a Jewish man, which was teaching us Polish, which actually was a good friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make many friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- there is one thing I've forgotten to tell you about the technical college, if I may go back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which stuck in my mind, is as the German armies were moving into Russia, the anti-Semitism become bigger and bigger. And in that school, in the technical college, I was going to school from 3 o'clock in the afternoon-- because it was in three shifts. Because there were so may kids, they couldn't accommodate, right? So it was three shifts, from 8:00 to 12:00, 12:00 to 3:00. I was from 3:00 till 8:00 in the evening. And it was a dark night, wintertime. And there was boys staying-- four or five boys was outside the sill. Because it was downstairs, you know, the school. And they were sharpening a knife. And they said, we are waiting for you, you [NON-ENGLISH], you know, and we're going to finish you off. And this I remember very well. And I got out of school. And I said, no, I'm going to stay. So there was a Jewish boy together with me. There was two other Jewish boys in the class. I said, let's go to the teacher and the headmaster will take us home. He said, no way I'm going out. I got out. And I-- by chance, I grabbed a half a brick and I threw it to one, and I hit the one in the head. And they run away. And after that night, next day, I went and bought myself a flick knife. And I had that flick knife, with my father's letters in here, and the flick knife in here, right through the war, even in Australia, until my uncle found it and threw it out. Pity-- my wife says a pity. It would have been a terrific memorabilia. But I had a knife in my pocket all through the war, right, from that point on. And they run away. And then, soon after, we found that place in the city. You know, my uncle found it and we moved to the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll take a break there.
